data.title: New office to assist SMEs in navigating complex regulations
data.description:
The office will be established under EnterpriseSG.

The Ministry of Trade and Industry will soon open a new office to help SMEs navigate regulations that are unclear or still being developed.

The Small and Medium-Sized Enterprises Pro-Enterprise Office (SME PEO), under Enterprise Singapore (EnterpriseSG), will begin operations on 26 March.

The SME PEO will allow SMEs to coordinate and direct their feedback or concerns to multiple public agencies for review.

The new office will also establish and track Whole-of-Government case management service standards on regulatory issues.

In addition, the SME PEO will analyse enquiries and feedback received, monitor resolution outcomes, and identify systemic regulatory issues.

data.title: Service sector growth to ease office vacancy in 2025
data.description:
Office vacancy in 2024 rose 9.4%.

Office vacancy inched up to 9.4% in 2024, primarily due to the addition of the 1.25-million-sq-ft IOI Central Boulevard Towers to office supply, Newmark reported.

Grade A office rents rose by approximately 2% in 2024 and are projected to grow at a similar pace in 2025, driven by limited new supply in the CBD and sustained demand for premium office space.

Meanwhile, service sectors such as information and communications, financial services, and professional services expanded in 2024 and are expected to grow further in 2025, supporting additional office demand.

"With a limited development pipeline and sustained need for high-quality office space, we anticipate that rental growth will remain positive in 2025 and occupiers will continue to focus on workplace efficiency and employee experience, driving interest for prime spaces,” Chris Carver, executive managing director and head of Asia-Pacific Valuation & Advisory at Newmark said.

data.title: H2G Green prices 3 million new shares at $0.04 apiece
data.description:
Trading of the new shares is expected to commence on or before 10 March.

H2G Green has allotted and issued 3 million new shares at $0.04 apiece on 6 March.

This issuance brings the total issued and paid-up share capital of H2G has increased from 1,451,968,092 shares to 1,454,968,092 shares.

The 3,000,000 new shares are expected to be listed and quoted on the Catalist Board of Singapore Exchange Securities Trading Limited (SGX-ST) on or around 10 March 2025.

Trading of the new shares is expected to commence with effect from 9.00 a.m. on the same date.

data.title: DBS CEO Piyush Gupta earned $17.58m in 2024
data.description:
About $9.35m of this is a deferred award, of which 17.1% will be in cash.

DBS CEO Piyush Gupta earned a total of S$17.58m for 2024.

A bulk of this— about $9.35m— is a deferred award, of which 17.1% will be in cash and the remainder will be in the form of shares.

His cash bonus for the period is $6.64m.

Meanwhile, $80,533 were non-cash components consisting of club, car, and driver.

Gupta is stepping down as CEO of DBS in 2025, after helming Singapore’s biggest bank by asset-size since 2008.

In 2023, Gupta had taken a $4.14m variable pay cut following tech disruptions.

data.title: Razorpay rolls out payments platform to Singapore
data.description:
It offers AI agents and a magic check-out service.

Razorpay has expanded its technology platform to Singapore.

The India-based fintech giant expanded into the Lion City following launching successful operations in Malaysia.

Businesses in Singapore currently pay 4% to 6% per transaction, fintech said. Razorpay promises to reduce cross-border transaction fees by 30% to 40% from the current market rate.

Razorpay’s payment platform in Singapore will also facilitate real-time payment rails like PayNow; Agentic-AI,  a software development kit (SDK) that enables AI agents to engage in transactions with human users; and RAY, an AI-concierge offering payment-related assistance such as payouts, payroll, and vendor payments.

Businesses can also reportedly enjoy enhanced operational efﬁciency with Magic Checkout by offering a one-click payment checkout experience, reducing drop-offs and abandoned transactions.

data.title: MAS bans credit use for cryptocurrency purchases
data.description:
MAS aims to help consumers avoid losses from crypto investments.

The Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) has prohibited digital payment token service providers from offering credit or leverage to all retail customers, regardless of age, for cryptocurrency purchases.

MAS has also restricted these providers from accepting credit cards.

According to MAS, cryptocurrencies are highly volatile and speculative, often lacking fundamental value. It has warned the public that dealing in cryptocurrencies is highly risky and not suitable for the general population.

Additionally, MAS stated that using credit or leverage magnifies losses, and investors can lose more than their initial investment.

MAS also cautioned that regulatory measures cannot shield consumers from cryptocurrency losses and advised them to avoid such investments.

data.title: ONE and Singapore Maritime Foundation ink talent enhancement deal
data.description:
Scholarships and internships will be offered.

Ocean Network Express (ONE) and the Singapore Maritime Foundation (SMF) have signed a memorandum of understanding to support talent development in the sector.

Under the deal, ONE will fund at least two MaritimeONE scholarships (including applicable overseas exchange programme) and two MaritimeONE internships per year over three years from 2025 to 2027.

These will be managed and marketed by SMF, with both parties jointly selecting the candidates.

This initiative is under SMF’s programmes under the MaritimeONE umbrella, which aims to connect youths and young adults with the maritime industry.

SMF Executive Director Tan Beng Tee said the partnership “comes at a pivotal juncture as the industry transitions into a future where technology is pervasive and shipping becomes greener, requiring a workforce with new skillsets.”

data.title: Government says no to separate COE category for private-hire cars
data.description:
The government introduced regulatory changes for taxis and PHC.

The government will not create a separate Certificate of Entitlement (COE) category for business-owned private-hire cars (PHCs).

“We have studied this carefully and decided not to proceed with such a move,” Senior Minister of State for Transport Dr Amy Khor said at the MOT Committee of Supply Debate 2025.

Khor said that most of the demand for Cat A and B COEs has been from local individuals, not PHC or car leasing companies. Khor cited that in the most recent bidding in February 2025, business-owned PHCs made up 6% and 8% of successful Cat A and B bids, respectively.

Khor also said that quota from existing categories, such as Cat A and B, would need to be taken away to provide the quota for the new separate category.

“We have seen that demand for PHCs varies from quarter to quarter, given that fares and driver supply are highly responsive to commuter demand. It is very difficult to accurately determine the COE quota supply for PHCs that is required to meet the needs of drivers and commuters,” Khor said.

The minister also said that moving too much quota away will increase prices for Cat A and B because of the inadequate remaining quota. On the other hand, moving to little will, COE prices for PHCs will be high, with drivers and commuters facing higher rental and fares.  

Instead, the government intend to strengthen regulatory regimes for taxis and PHCs. After suspending the growth cap on the taxi fleet, the government plans to allow taxi operators to convert used vehicles that are less than five years old to taxis. Instead of just buying new vehicles, taxi operators will have an additional option to grow their fleet, which may help reduce costs.

It will also allow taxi operators to resell taxis that are more than three years old, capped at 5% of their fleet annually. This will give taxi operators flexibility to resell under-utilised taxis and reduce carrying costs. Khor said this added flexibility can also reduce operators’ risks when trialling new taxi models, such as bigger, more expensive multi-purpose vehicles which can better serve larger families.

“These moves give taxi operators more flexibility to manage cost and grow their fleet. They complement our moves in the PHC sector to further level the playing field between taxis and PHCs,” Khor said.

data.title: Mediacorp appoints Susan Ho to board of directors
data.description:
She held senior leadership roles at Standard Chartered Bank, Brunswick, and fintech unicorn Airwallex.

Mediacorp has appointed Susan Ho to its Board of Directors, effective 1 March.

Ho has over 30 years of experience in international branding, corporate strategy, and reputation management.

She served as Global Head of Brand & Sponsorships at Standard Chartered Bank; and held senior leadership roles at corporate advisory firm Brunswick and global fintech unicorn Airwallex.

She started her career in advertising and digital marketing before transitioning into corporate banking and advisory.

data.title: JTC extends industrial land leases to boost business growth
data.description:
JTC will extend lease tenures to cover the development phase to ensure businesses enjoy the full 20 or 30-year lease term.

Businesses in Singapore’s industrial sector will benefit from longer lease tenures and increased flexibility under new enhancements to the Industrial Land Lease Framework announced by JTC.

The changes, aimed at improving investment viability and business growth, include longer leases for new developments, a new lease extension scheme, earlier renewal options, and expanded investment recognition.

One of the most significant enhancements is the addition of three extra years to all new greenfield industrial land leases.

Businesses have raised concerns that up to three years are spent on land development, reducing the actual productive lease period.

To address this, JTC will extend lease tenures to cover the development phase, ensuring businesses enjoy the full 20 or 30-year lease term. This measure takes immediate effect.

Another major improvement is the introduction of the Flexible Lease Extension Initiative (FLEXI) scheme, which will allow eligible businesses on 20-year JTC leases to extend their tenure by up to 10 additional years in two five-year tranches.

Companies must demonstrate strong economic performance and commit to new plant and machinery (P&M) investments to qualify. The FLEXI scheme will launch in the second half of 2025.

To provide greater certainty in long-term business planning, JTC is bringing forward the lease renewal application window. Instead of waiting until six years before lease expiry, companies can now apply ten years in advance.

This change, also set for implementation in 2H2025, gives businesses more time to plan expansion and investment strategies.

JTC is also expanding the definition of P&M investments to recognize innovation, research & development (R&D), digital transformation, and Intellectual Property (IP) creation.
